title: Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis
oneLiner: Chronic cholestatic liver disease with progressive bile duct inflammation/fibrosis, strongly associated with IBD; leads to cirrhosis, cholangiocarcinoma. (20 words)

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 35-year-old man with known ulcerative colitis (pancolitis, in remission) presents with progressive pruritus (worse at night, no rash), fatigue, and intermittent right upper quadrant pain. He has had two episodes of "unexplained" cholangitis over the past year. LFTs show an **elevated ALP** out of proportion to ALT, with mildly raised bilirubin. He is jaundiced. MRCP reveals multifocal strictures and beading of intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts. P-ANCA is positive.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Young male + UC** → classic demographic (70% male, peak age 30-40, 70-80% have IBD – usually UC, less often Crohn’s)
- **Pruritus** → hallmark of cholestasis (bile salt accumulation in skin). No rash differentiates from primary biliary cholangitis (xanthelasmas, xanthomas) or allergic cause.
- **ALP >> ALT** → **cholestatic** pattern (ALP from damaged cholangiocytes, not hepatocytes)
- **Recurrent cholangitis** without stones → strictures causing biliary obstruction and bacterial superinfection
- **MRCP beading** → irregular strictures alternating with dilatations: the **classic "beaded" appearance** of PSC
- **P-ANCA positive** → non-specific autoimmune marker, seen in ~80% of PSC (but also in IBD alone; not diagnostic in isolation)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **Asymptomatic with incidental ALP elevation** – patient has no symptoms; discovered on routine LFTs. Don't brush off: many have silent disease until late stage.
2. **Decompensated cirrhosis first** – presents with variceal bleed, ascites, or encephalopathy without prior jaundice or pruritus. *Think PSC if young, no alcohol, and liver biopsy shows onion-skin fibrosis.*
3. **Cholangiocarcinoma (CCA) as first presentation** – painless jaundice, weight loss, raised CA19-9, dominant stricture on ERCP. *Always suspect PSC in a young patient with CCA even without known diagnosis.*

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** Primary sclerosing cholangitis is a chronic, progressive disease where the immune system attacks the bile ducts inside and outside the liver, causing scarring (sclerosis) that narrows the ducts. Bile backs up (cholestasis), damages liver cells, and eventually leads to cirrhosis.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Uncommon (prevalence ~1-16 per 100,000) but you will see it if you work with IBD – up to 5% of UC patients develop PSC.
- **Stakes:** 50% of patients progress to liver transplant or death within 10-20 years. **Cholangiocarcinoma** risk is 10-15% lifetime – 400x general population. Screening is difficult.
- **Common pitfalls:** (1) Misdiagnosing as primary biliary cholangitis (anti-mitochondrial antibody negative here, pruritus + ALP similar but AMA+ in PBC). (2) Ignoring "innocent" ALP elevation in an IBD patient. (3) Delaying MRCP because symptoms are "vague" – by the time cirrhosis appears, transplant window may narrow.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**Story of development:**
PSC begins in the small bile ducts (interlobular and septal). There is a **lymphocytic infiltration** of the duct epithelium – probably triggered by a combination of genetic susceptibility (HLA haplotypes; **DRB1*0301**, **DRB1*1301**) and an environmental trigger (possibly gut bacteria crossing a permeable IBD mucosa via the enterohepatic circulation). This causes **chronic inflammation** → **periductal fibrosis** → **"onion-skin" fibrosis** (concentric layers of collagen). The duct becomes stenotic, and upstream bile stasis leads to further damage, cholestasis, and eventually **secondary biliary cirrhosis** (biliary pattern of cirrhosis).

**Why each key sign happens:**
- **Elevated ALP:** ALP is produced by cholangiocytes and released into serum when bile ducts are damaged/inflamed. It is *far* more specific to ductal damage than ALT (hepatocyte). That's why ALP is disproportionately high.
- **Pruritus:** Bile salts (especially ursodeoxycholic acid and chenodeoxycholic acid) accumulate in serum and deposit in the skin. Binding to TGR5 receptors on sensory neurons triggers itch. It's *not* due to bilirubin – patients can have severe itch with normal bilirubin.
- **Jaundice / hyperbilirubinemia:** Only when a dominant stricture or extensive intrahepatic involvement obstructs enough ducts to cause conjugated bilirubin backflow. Often a late sign or an acute-on-chronic (cholangitis) event.
- **Recurrent cholangitis:** Bile is normally sterile. Strictures create stagnation; bacteria from the small intestine travel up the duct (especially after ERCP or sphincterotomy) and cause ascending cholangitis – fever, right upper quadrant pain, jaundice (Charcot's triad).
- **Fatigue:** Multifactorial – cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6) from chronic inflammation, sleep disturbance from pruritus, and possibly mitochondrial dysfunction in cholestasis. Prograde failure, not simple fatigue.

**Connect to pattern recognition:** The "beaded" MRCP appearance is *strictures* (scarred ducts) alternating with *dilations* (areas of bile accumulation behind the stricture). Onion-skin fibrosis seen on biopsy is pathognomonic but rarely needed now (MRCP + clinical picture suffices).

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue / Pattern |
|----------|-------|---------------------|
| **Idiopathic** | **Primary** – no single cause; most cases | No known trigger; strong IBD association |
| **Autoimmune / Genetic** | HLA associations (DRB1*0301, DRB1*1301); family history | IBD patient, especially with HLA B8/DR3 |
| **Infectious** | Proposed: *cytomegalovirus, reovirus, *Cryptosporidium** in immunocompromised? (weak evidence) | Usually not relevant in typical PSC |
| **Secondary** (mimics PSC) | IgG4-related disease, eosinophilic cholangitis, surgical trauma, **ischemic cholangiopathy** (post-liver transplant, critical illness) | Look for IgG4 elevation, history of surgery, shock |
| **Drug-induced** (rare) | Intrahepatic strictures: *floxuridine*, *taxanes* | Chemotherapy history with hepatic arterial infusion |
| **Most common cause** | **Primary** (idiopathic) – but all patients with PSC require exclusion of secondary causes, especially IgG4-related disease (which is treatable). |

**Key takeaway:** In every "PSC" patient, think: *Is this primary or secondary?* A positive anti-mitochondrial antibody means PBC, not PSC. Elevated IgG4 means IgG4-related sclerosing cholangitis – often responds to steroids (unlike PSC). A history of recurrent pyogenic cholangitis or HIV/AIDS points to infection-related strictures.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**
- **Gold standard:** MRCP (magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography) showing multifocal strictures and dilations of intra- and/or extrahepatic bile ducts, in the **absence of secondary causes** (no prior biliary surgery, no choledocholithiasis, no IgG4 disease, no AIDS cholangiopathy).
- **Supporting labs:** Cholestatic LFTs (elevated ALP, GGT), often with minimal transaminitis. P-ANCA (atypical anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody) positive in ~80%, but not specific. pANCA (anti-MPO) is negative; it's atypical.
- **Liver biopsy:** Usually not required for diagnosis unless small-duct PSC suspected (normal MRCP but cholestasis + IBD). Shows **concentric periductal fibrosis** (onion-skinning), ductopenia, and fibrous obliteration of small ducts.

**Test details:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **LFTs (ALP, GGT, ALT, AST, bilirubin)** | Cholestatic vs hepatocellular pattern | Initial screen | ALP/GGT disproportionate rise suggests bile duct damage. ALT <5x upper limit of normal. |
| **MRCP** | Bile duct anatomy: strictures, dilations, beading | Non-invasive gold standard | Multifocal intrahepatic and/or extrahepatic strictures. Dominant stricture (≥1.5mm in CBD) raises concern for cholangiocarcinoma. |
| **ERCP** | Same as MRCP but therapeutic potential | If MRCP equivocal or if need for biopsy/sphincterotomy of dominant stricture | Also yields brushings for cytology to exclude malignancy. Higher complication rate (pancreatitis, perforation). |
| **Serum IgG4** | Elevated in IgG4-related disease | Rule out mimic | >140 mg/dL suggests IgG4-SC. |
| **Anti-mitochondrial antibody (AMA)** | Negative in PSC; positive in PBC | Rule out PBC | If positive, diagnosis is PBC not PSC (though rare overlap exists). |
| **CA19-9** | Tumor marker for cholangiocarcinoma | Screening (controversial) | >100 U/mL in PSC patient raises suspicion for CCA, but can be elevated in cholestasis. Not specific. |
| **Liver biopsy** | Histology: onion-skin fibrosis | Only if small-duct PSC suspected or for staging | Stage 1: portal hepatitis; 2: periportal fibrosis; 3: septal fibrosis; 4: cirrhosis. |

**Single finding that confirms:** MRCP showing beaded intra/extrahepatic ducts in a patient with IBD and cholestatic LFTs, after excluding secondary causes. **Single finding that rules out:** Normal MRCP with no cholestasis and no IBD (though small-duct PSC can be missed).

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**  
- **If acute cholangitis:** Charcot's triad → sepsis. **Cefotaxime 2g IV** (or piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV) after cultures. Fluids. Urgent ERCP with biliary drainage (sphincterotomy ± stent) if no response to antibiotics within 12-24h. Do NOT delay ERCP for stones? No – strictures are the cause, not stones.
- **If dominant stricture with jaundice but no infection:** Elective ERCP with brushings for cytology. Empiric antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1g IV) before procedure.

**Ward (24-72h):**  
- **Pruritus:** First line: **ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) 13-15 mg/kg/day PO** in divided doses (or once daily). *But:* evidence is mixed – may improve LFTs but does not delay time to transplant or death. High doses (28-30 mg/kg) actually *increase* risk of adverse outcomes. Do not use high-dose UDCA.
- Second-line pruritus: **cholestyramine 4g PO q6h** (bile acid sequestrant – binds bile salts in gut, reduces pruritus). Monitor for fat-soluble vitamin deficiency.
- **If pruritus refractory:** Consider naltrexone, rifampicin, sertraline (off-label, specialist only).
- **Vitamin replacement:** Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are deficient due to cholestasis. **Multivitamin with fat-soluble vitamins** PO daily; if bilirubin >3 mg/dL, give **vitamin K 10mg IM monthly** (prevents coagulopathy).
- **IBD control:** Optimize UC treatment if active; but note: PSC can progress independently of IBD activity. Avoid hepatotoxic drugs (azathioprine may be OK, but monitor). **5-ASA** is safe. **Infliximab** not contraindicated.

**Long-term:**  
- **Surveillance:** **Annual MRCP + CA19-9** for cholangiocarcinoma (controversial, but offered in most centres). Dominant stricture on MRCP → ERCP with brushings. If brushings equivocal, consider FISH (fluorescence in situ hybridization) for polysomy.
- **Osteoporosis prophylaxis:** **Vitamin D 800 IU + calcium 1000 mg PO daily** – PSC patients have reduced bone density due to cholestasis, vitamin D deficiency, and possibly IBD steroids.
- **Portal hypertension management:** If cirrhosis develops: screen for varices (OGD), measure MELD score, consider liver transplant evaluation.
- **Transplant indication:** Decompensated cirrhosis (ascites, variceal bleeding, encephalopathy), recurrent cholangitis, or early cholangiocarcinoma (selected cases). **PSC recurs in 20-30%** of transplanted livers within 10 years, so recurrent strictures post-transplant should trigger suspicion.
- **What NOT to do:**
  - Do not use **high-dose UDCA** (≥28 mg/kg) – increased mortality, need for transplant.
  - Do not give **steroids** (not effective; only in IgG4-related disease).
  - Do not perform **routine ERCP** just for diagnosis – MRCP is safer.
  - Do not ignore **dominant stricture** – it could be cholangiocarcinoma.

**Common pitfalls:**
- Assuming all bile duct abnormalities in IBD are PSC – could be IgG4-SC (treatable with steroids) or secondary to ERCP trauma.
- Forgetting to check vitamin D and bone density – osteoporosis is common and silent.
- Not referring for transplant early enough – once decompensated, survival without transplant is <2 years.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | **Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis** | **Primary Biliary Cholangitis** | **IgG4-Related Sclerosing Cholangitis** | **Cholangiocarcinoma (hilar) — dominant stricture** |
|---------|-----------------------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| Demographics | Young to middle-aged men (70%), UC | Middle-aged women (90% F, age 40-60) | Older men (60s, 2:1 M:F) | Older, >60, male > female |
| Pain | RUQ mild, intermittent | Often none; RUQ pain if advanced | Jaundice, weight loss, abdominal pain | Painless jaundice (often) |
| Exam | Hepatomegaly, jaundice late | Xanthelasmas, hyperpigmentation, hepatomegaly | Painless jaundice, may have pancreatitis | Palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier's sign if distal; hilar may not) |
| LFTs | ALP >> ALT, bilirubin varies | ALP >> ALT, bilirubin rises late | Cholestatic pattern similar to PSC | Cholestatic with high bilirubin |
| AMA | Negative | **Positive** (95%) | Negative | Negative |
| IgG4 | Normal | Normal | **Elevated** (>140 mg/dL) | Normal |
| MRCP | Multifocal beaded strictures, intra/extrahepatic | Small intrahepatic duct loss, normal extrahepatic | Diffuse or focal strictures; **thickened bile duct wall** | Solitary dominant stricture, often hilar, with mass lesion on MRI |
| IBD association | **Strong** (70-80%) | Weak | Weak (up to 10% UC) | None |
| Key differentiating test | MRCP + IBD history + IgG4 normal + AMA- | AMA+ | High IgG4, rapid response to steroids | Brushings cytology + FISH polysomy; biopsy of mass |
| Response to UDCA | Modest ALP improvement, no survival benefit | Slows disease progression (improves ALP, transplant-free survival) | Steroids: excellent | None |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Cholangiocarcinoma (CCA)** | Rapid jaundice, weight loss, pruritus, CA19-9 >100; dominant stricture on MRCP worsens | Jaundice, palpable gallbladder, hepatomegaly if metastatic | ERCP brushings + FISH. If resectable: surgery. If not: stenting, palliative chemo. **Lifetime risk 10-15%.** |
| **Recurrent cholangitis** | Charcot's triad: fever, RUQ pain, jaundice; may progress to Reynolds' pentad (+ shock + confusion) | High fever, rigors, hypotension, tender RUQ | IV antibiotics + urgent ERCP drainage. Prevent with UDCA? No. |
| **Cirrhosis / portal HTN** | Ascites, variceal bleeding, encephalopathy, splenomegaly, low platelets | Spider naevi, caput medusae, flapping tremor | OGD screen varices; banding. Non-selective beta-blockers. Transplant evaluation. |
| **Fat-soluble vitamin deficiency** | Night blindness (vitamin A), easy bruising (K), osteomalacia (D), neurological symptoms (E) | Prolonged INR, low vitamin A, D, E levels | Replace: vitamin K 10 mg IM monthly, ADEK multivitamin PO. |
| **Hepatocellular carcinoma** | Rare (as opposed to HCV/HBV cirrhosis) – more often CCA | Elevated AFP? Not typical | Surveillance with ultrasound q6-12 months if cirrhotic. |
| **Dominant stricture** (benign vs malignant) | Worsening jaundice, pruritus, cholangitis | Sudden increase in bilirubin >2 mg/dL within weeks | ERCP + brushings for cytology. Stent if benign. |
| **Gallbladder / bile duct stones** | RUQ pain, pancreatitis, cholangitis | Usually pigment stones due to chronic cholestasis | Cholecystectomy if symptomatic, but beware of coexisting cirrhosis. |

**Late-presentation patterns:** An untreated PSC patient may present with *end-stage liver disease* without ever having had jaundice or pruritus (silent progression). The stem might describe a 45-year-old with UC who found a new abdominal wall varix and has splenomegaly. The LFTs show low albumin, prolonged INR, and cholestasis. *Don't miss the cirrhosis.* Another stem: a patient with known PSC who stops attending follow-up, then presents with *acute cholangitis and coma* (hepatic encephalopathy from sepsis). High mortality.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**
- **ABBC** – for strictures: **A**utoimmune (PSC, IgG4), **B**acterial (recurrent pyogenic cholangitis), **B**enign (ischemic, surgical trauma), **C**ancer (cholangiocarcinoma, pancreatic cancer). *Use this to avoid anchoring on PSC.*
- **PSC:** **P**ancreatobiliary **S**trictures **C**holangitis – but primary is *intra + extrahepatic, not just biliary.*
- **SAME:** **S**trictures, **A**LP ↑, **M**iddle-aged men, **E**xclude IgG4.

**Clinical Pearls (pattern recognition):**
1. **Pruritus in a patient with IBD is PSC until proven otherwise** – check ALP and MRCP.
2. **ALP elevation >10x normal with ALT <100 in an IBD patient?** The odds are >90% PSC (or primary biliary cholangitis if AMA positive).
3. **A "dominant stricture" (CBD >1.5mm on MRCP) in a known PSC patient is malignant until proven innocent** – needs ERCP brushings every 6-12 months.
4. **If a patient with PSC gets jaundice without cholangitis, think dominant stricture or CCA** – not just progression of disease.
5. **PSC can present years before IBD** – always ask about bowel symptoms, but don't rely on them. A normal colonoscopy doesn't exclude future IBD.
6. **Small-duct PSC (normal MRCP, biopsy positive) has a **better prognosis** than classic PSC** – lower risk of CCA, less need for transplant.
7. **Liver biopsy is rarely needed for PSC diagnosis** – reserve for small-duct suspicion or when MRCP is contraindicated.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- **Ordering ERCP "for diagnosis"** – use MRCP first. ERCP carries 10-30% risk of pancreatitis in PSC patients (papilla manipulation).
- **Giving high-dose UDCA** (already covered – dangerous).
- **Treating itch with antihistamines** (ineffective) instead of cholestyramine or UDCA.
- **Using oral vitamin K when coagulopathy is due to liver failure, not deficiency** – check INR response after IM vitamin K.
- **Missing CCA** – a rise in CA19-9 in a PSC patient is not always cancer, but >100 warrants prompt MRCP/ERCP.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior or ICU:**
- **NEWS2 ≥ 7** (or ≥5 with any single red parameter) – concern for cholangitis/sepsis
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** – risk of septic shock
- **Systolic BP <90 mmHg** despite 30 mL/kg fluid bolus → possible ascending cholangitis with septic shock → ICU
- **GCS ≤14** or acute confusion → hepatic encephalopathy or sepsis-associated delirium
- **Acute variceal bleed:** massive hematemesis or melena, tachycardia, hypotension → call gastro/bleeding team, massive transfusion protocol, ICU if unstable.
- **Rising bilirubin >10 mg/dL in <2 weeks** with right upper quadrant pain and fever → urgent ERCP needed (call hepatobiliary consultant)
- **Anuria or creatinine >250 µmol/L** – hepatorenal syndrome (type 1) is an ICU indication for vasopressors and possibly terlipressin/albumin.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **MELD score >15** → consider transplant referral; >20 → urgent transplant evaluation.
- **INR >2.5** despite vitamin K → liver failure, not deficiency; call senior.
- **Platelets <50 x10⁹/L** with active bleeding → platelet transfusion + consider splenic artery embolization if hypersplenism.
- **Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP):** ascitic fluid PMN >250 cells/µL → IV albumin + antibiotics.

**Remember:** PSC patients are immunosuppressed if they have IBD on biologics or post-transplant – lower threshold for sepsis. Any decompensation (acute on chronic liver failure) should trigger immediate multidisciplinary team (hepatology, transplant surgery, ICU) discussion.

---

**Final teaching points:** PSC is not just a liver disease – it's a systemic condition with bowel, bone, and biliary malignancy risks. Your job as a junior doctor is to recognise the pattern early, avoid harmful therapies (high-dose UDCA, unnecessary ERCP), ensure vitamin supplementation, and keep a low threshold for cholangiocarcinoma surveillance. The two words to remember: **stagnation and stricture.** Keep the bile flowing – because stagnation breeds infection and cancer.

rawMarkdown:
## Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis

**One-liner:** Chronic cholestatic liver disease with progressive bile duct inflammation/fibrosis, strongly associated with IBD; leads to cirrhosis, cholangiocarcinoma. (20 words)

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:** A 35-year-old man with known ulcerative colitis (pancolitis, in remission) presents with progressive pruritus (worse at night, no rash), fatigue, and intermittent right upper quadrant pain. He has had two episodes of "unexplained" cholangitis over the past year. LFTs show an **elevated ALP** out of proportion to ALT, with mildly raised bilirubin. He is jaundiced. MRCP reveals multifocal strictures and beading of intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts. P-ANCA is positive.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Young male + UC** → classic demographic (70% male, peak age 30-40, 70-80% have IBD – usually UC, less often Crohn’s)
- **Pruritus** → hallmark of cholestasis (bile salt accumulation in skin). No rash differentiates from primary biliary cholangitis (xanthelasmas, xanthomas) or allergic cause.
- **ALP >> ALT** → **cholestatic** pattern (ALP from damaged cholangiocytes, not hepatocytes)
- **Recurrent cholangitis** without stones → strictures causing biliary obstruction and bacterial superinfection
- **MRCP beading** → irregular strictures alternating with dilatations: the **classic "beaded" appearance** of PSC
- **P-ANCA positive** → non-specific autoimmune marker, seen in ~80% of PSC (but also in IBD alone; not diagnostic in isolation)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **Asymptomatic with incidental ALP elevation** – patient has no symptoms; discovered on routine LFTs. Don't brush off: many have silent disease until late stage.
2. **Decompensated cirrhosis first** – presents with variceal bleed, ascites, or encephalopathy without prior jaundice or pruritus. *Think PSC if young, no alcohol, and liver biopsy shows onion-skin fibrosis.*
3. **Cholangiocarcinoma (CCA) as first presentation** – painless jaundice, weight loss, raised CA19-9, dominant stricture on ERCP. *Always suspect PSC in a young patient with CCA even without known diagnosis.*

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:** Primary sclerosing cholangitis is a chronic, progressive disease where the immune system attacks the bile ducts inside and outside the liver, causing scarring (sclerosis) that narrows the ducts. Bile backs up (cholestasis), damages liver cells, and eventually leads to cirrhosis.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Uncommon (prevalence ~1-16 per 100,000) but you will see it if you work with IBD – up to 5% of UC patients develop PSC.
- **Stakes:** 50% of patients progress to liver transplant or death within 10-20 years. **Cholangiocarcinoma** risk is 10-15% lifetime – 400x general population. Screening is difficult.
- **Common pitfalls:** (1) Misdiagnosing as primary biliary cholangitis (anti-mitochondrial antibody negative here, pruritus + ALP similar but AMA+ in PBC). (2) Ignoring "innocent" ALP elevation in an IBD patient. (3) Delaying MRCP because symptoms are "vague" – by the time cirrhosis appears, transplant window may narrow.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**Story of development:**
PSC begins in the small bile ducts (interlobular and septal). There is a **lymphocytic infiltration** of the duct epithelium – probably triggered by a combination of genetic susceptibility (HLA haplotypes; **DRB1*0301**, **DRB1*1301**) and an environmental trigger (possibly gut bacteria crossing a permeable IBD mucosa via the enterohepatic circulation). This causes **chronic inflammation** → **periductal fibrosis** → **"onion-skin" fibrosis** (concentric layers of collagen). The duct becomes stenotic, and upstream bile stasis leads to further damage, cholestasis, and eventually **secondary biliary cirrhosis** (biliary pattern of cirrhosis).

**Why each key sign happens:**
- **Elevated ALP:** ALP is produced by cholangiocytes and released into serum when bile ducts are damaged/inflamed. It is *far* more specific to ductal damage than ALT (hepatocyte). That's why ALP is disproportionately high.
- **Pruritus:** Bile salts (especially ursodeoxycholic acid and chenodeoxycholic acid) accumulate in serum and deposit in the skin. Binding to TGR5 receptors on sensory neurons triggers itch. It's *not* due to bilirubin – patients can have severe itch with normal bilirubin.
- **Jaundice / hyperbilirubinemia:** Only when a dominant stricture or extensive intrahepatic involvement obstructs enough ducts to cause conjugated bilirubin backflow. Often a late sign or an acute-on-chronic (cholangitis) event.
- **Recurrent cholangitis:** Bile is normally sterile. Strictures create stagnation; bacteria from the small intestine travel up the duct (especially after ERCP or sphincterotomy) and cause ascending cholangitis – fever, right upper quadrant pain, jaundice (Charcot's triad).
- **Fatigue:** Multifactorial – cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6) from chronic inflammation, sleep disturbance from pruritus, and possibly mitochondrial dysfunction in cholestasis. Prograde failure, not simple fatigue.

**Connect to pattern recognition:** The "beaded" MRCP appearance is *strictures* (scarred ducts) alternating with *dilations* (areas of bile accumulation behind the stricture). Onion-skin fibrosis seen on biopsy is pathognomonic but rarely needed now (MRCP + clinical picture suffices).

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue / Pattern |
|----------|-------|---------------------|
| **Idiopathic** | **Primary** – no single cause; most cases | No known trigger; strong IBD association |
| **Autoimmune / Genetic** | HLA associations (DRB1*0301, DRB1*1301); family history | IBD patient, especially with HLA B8/DR3 |
| **Infectious** | Proposed: *cytomegalovirus, reovirus, *Cryptosporidium** in immunocompromised? (weak evidence) | Usually not relevant in typical PSC |
| **Secondary** (mimics PSC) | IgG4-related disease, eosinophilic cholangitis, surgical trauma, **ischemic cholangiopathy** (post-liver transplant, critical illness) | Look for IgG4 elevation, history of surgery, shock |
| **Drug-induced** (rare) | Intrahepatic strictures: *floxuridine*, *taxanes* | Chemotherapy history with hepatic arterial infusion |
| **Most common cause** | **Primary** (idiopathic) – but all patients with PSC require exclusion of secondary causes, especially IgG4-related disease (which is treatable). |

**Key takeaway:** In every "PSC" patient, think: *Is this primary or secondary?* A positive anti-mitochondrial antibody means PBC, not PSC. Elevated IgG4 means IgG4-related sclerosing cholangitis – often responds to steroids (unlike PSC). A history of recurrent pyogenic cholangitis or HIV/AIDS points to infection-related strictures.

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:**
- **Gold standard:** MRCP (magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography) showing multifocal strictures and dilations of intra- and/or extrahepatic bile ducts, in the **absence of secondary causes** (no prior biliary surgery, no choledocholithiasis, no IgG4 disease, no AIDS cholangiopathy).
- **Supporting labs:** Cholestatic LFTs (elevated ALP, GGT), often with minimal transaminitis. P-ANCA (atypical anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody) positive in ~80%, but not specific. pANCA (anti-MPO) is negative; it's atypical.
- **Liver biopsy:** Usually not required for diagnosis unless small-duct PSC suspected (normal MRCP but cholestasis + IBD). Shows **concentric periductal fibrosis** (onion-skinning), ductopenia, and fibrous obliteration of small ducts.

**Test details:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **LFTs (ALP, GGT, ALT, AST, bilirubin)** | Cholestatic vs hepatocellular pattern | Initial screen | ALP/GGT disproportionate rise suggests bile duct damage. ALT <5x upper limit of normal. |
| **MRCP** | Bile duct anatomy: strictures, dilations, beading | Non-invasive gold standard | Multifocal intrahepatic and/or extrahepatic strictures. Dominant stricture (≥1.5mm in CBD) raises concern for cholangiocarcinoma. |
| **ERCP** | Same as MRCP but therapeutic potential | If MRCP equivocal or if need for biopsy/sphincterotomy of dominant stricture | Also yields brushings for cytology to exclude malignancy. Higher complication rate (pancreatitis, perforation). |
| **Serum IgG4** | Elevated in IgG4-related disease | Rule out mimic | >140 mg/dL suggests IgG4-SC. |
| **Anti-mitochondrial antibody (AMA)** | Negative in PSC; positive in PBC | Rule out PBC | If positive, diagnosis is PBC not PSC (though rare overlap exists). |
| **CA19-9** | Tumor marker for cholangiocarcinoma | Screening (controversial) | >100 U/mL in PSC patient raises suspicion for CCA, but can be elevated in cholestasis. Not specific. |
| **Liver biopsy** | Histology: onion-skin fibrosis | Only if small-duct PSC suspected or for staging | Stage 1: portal hepatitis; 2: periportal fibrosis; 3: septal fibrosis; 4: cirrhosis. |

**Single finding that confirms:** MRCP showing beaded intra/extrahepatic ducts in a patient with IBD and cholestatic LFTs, after excluding secondary causes. **Single finding that rules out:** Normal MRCP with no cholestasis and no IBD (though small-duct PSC can be missed).

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**  
- **If acute cholangitis:** Charcot's triad → sepsis. **Cefotaxime 2g IV** (or piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV) after cultures. Fluids. Urgent ERCP with biliary drainage (sphincterotomy ± stent) if no response to antibiotics within 12-24h. Do NOT delay ERCP for stones? No – strictures are the cause, not stones.
- **If dominant stricture with jaundice but no infection:** Elective ERCP with brushings for cytology. Empiric antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1g IV) before procedure.

**Ward (24-72h):**  
- **Pruritus:** First line: **ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) 13-15 mg/kg/day PO** in divided doses (or once daily). *But:* evidence is mixed – may improve LFTs but does not delay time to transplant or death. High doses (28-30 mg/kg) actually *increase* risk of adverse outcomes. Do not use high-dose UDCA.
- Second-line pruritus: **cholestyramine 4g PO q6h** (bile acid sequestrant – binds bile salts in gut, reduces pruritus). Monitor for fat-soluble vitamin deficiency.
- **If pruritus refractory:** Consider naltrexone, rifampicin, sertraline (off-label, specialist only).
- **Vitamin replacement:** Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are deficient due to cholestasis. **Multivitamin with fat-soluble vitamins** PO daily; if bilirubin >3 mg/dL, give **vitamin K 10mg IM monthly** (prevents coagulopathy).
- **IBD control:** Optimize UC treatment if active; but note: PSC can progress independently of IBD activity. Avoid hepatotoxic drugs (azathioprine may be OK, but monitor). **5-ASA** is safe. **Infliximab** not contraindicated.

**Long-term:**  
- **Surveillance:** **Annual MRCP + CA19-9** for cholangiocarcinoma (controversial, but offered in most centres). Dominant stricture on MRCP → ERCP with brushings. If brushings equivocal, consider FISH (fluorescence in situ hybridization) for polysomy.
- **Osteoporosis prophylaxis:** **Vitamin D 800 IU + calcium 1000 mg PO daily** – PSC patients have reduced bone density due to cholestasis, vitamin D deficiency, and possibly IBD steroids.
- **Portal hypertension management:** If cirrhosis develops: screen for varices (OGD), measure MELD score, consider liver transplant evaluation.
- **Transplant indication:** Decompensated cirrhosis (ascites, variceal bleeding, encephalopathy), recurrent cholangitis, or early cholangiocarcinoma (selected cases). **PSC recurs in 20-30%** of transplanted livers within 10 years, so recurrent strictures post-transplant should trigger suspicion.
- **What NOT to do:**
  - Do not use **high-dose UDCA** (≥28 mg/kg) – increased mortality, need for transplant.
  - Do not give **steroids** (not effective; only in IgG4-related disease).
  - Do not perform **routine ERCP** just for diagnosis – MRCP is safer.
  - Do not ignore **dominant stricture** – it could be cholangiocarcinoma.

**Common pitfalls:**
- Assuming all bile duct abnormalities in IBD are PSC – could be IgG4-SC (treatable with steroids) or secondary to ERCP trauma.
- Forgetting to check vitamin D and bone density – osteoporosis is common and silent.
- Not referring for transplant early enough – once decompensated, survival without transplant is <2 years.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | **Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis** | **Primary Biliary Cholangitis** | **IgG4-Related Sclerosing Cholangitis** | **Cholangiocarcinoma (hilar) — dominant stricture** |
|---------|-----------------------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| Demographics | Young to middle-aged men (70%), UC | Middle-aged women (90% F, age 40-60) | Older men (60s, 2:1 M:F) | Older, >60, male > female |
| Pain | RUQ mild, intermittent | Often none; RUQ pain if advanced | Jaundice, weight loss, abdominal pain | Painless jaundice (often) |
| Exam | Hepatomegaly, jaundice late | Xanthelasmas, hyperpigmentation, hepatomegaly | Painless jaundice, may have pancreatitis | Palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier's sign if distal; hilar may not) |
| LFTs | ALP >> ALT, bilirubin varies | ALP >> ALT, bilirubin rises late | Cholestatic pattern similar to PSC | Cholestatic with high bilirubin |
| AMA | Negative | **Positive** (95%) | Negative | Negative |
| IgG4 | Normal | Normal | **Elevated** (>140 mg/dL) | Normal |
| MRCP | Multifocal beaded strictures, intra/extrahepatic | Small intrahepatic duct loss, normal extrahepatic | Diffuse or focal strictures; **thickened bile duct wall** | Solitary dominant stricture, often hilar, with mass lesion on MRI |
| IBD association | **Strong** (70-80%) | Weak | Weak (up to 10% UC) | None |
| Key differentiating test | MRCP + IBD history + IgG4 normal + AMA- | AMA+ | High IgG4, rapid response to steroids | Brushings cytology + FISH polysomy; biopsy of mass |
| Response to UDCA | Modest ALP improvement, no survival benefit | Slows disease progression (improves ALP, transplant-free survival) | Steroids: excellent | None |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Cholangiocarcinoma (CCA)** | Rapid jaundice, weight loss, pruritus, CA19-9 >100; dominant stricture on MRCP worsens | Jaundice, palpable gallbladder, hepatomegaly if metastatic | ERCP brushings + FISH. If resectable: surgery. If not: stenting, palliative chemo. **Lifetime risk 10-15%.** |
| **Recurrent cholangitis** | Charcot's triad: fever, RUQ pain, jaundice; may progress to Reynolds' pentad (+ shock + confusion) | High fever, rigors, hypotension, tender RUQ | IV antibiotics + urgent ERCP drainage. Prevent with UDCA? No. |
| **Cirrhosis / portal HTN** | Ascites, variceal bleeding, encephalopathy, splenomegaly, low platelets | Spider naevi, caput medusae, flapping tremor | OGD screen varices; banding. Non-selective beta-blockers. Transplant evaluation. |
| **Fat-soluble vitamin deficiency** | Night blindness (vitamin A), easy bruising (K), osteomalacia (D), neurological symptoms (E) | Prolonged INR, low vitamin A, D, E levels | Replace: vitamin K 10 mg IM monthly, ADEK multivitamin PO. |
| **Hepatocellular carcinoma** | Rare (as opposed to HCV/HBV cirrhosis) – more often CCA | Elevated AFP? Not typical | Surveillance with ultrasound q6-12 months if cirrhotic. |
| **Dominant stricture** (benign vs malignant) | Worsening jaundice, pruritus, cholangitis | Sudden increase in bilirubin >2 mg/dL within weeks | ERCP + brushings for cytology. Stent if benign. |
| **Gallbladder / bile duct stones** | RUQ pain, pancreatitis, cholangitis | Usually pigment stones due to chronic cholestasis | Cholecystectomy if symptomatic, but beware of coexisting cirrhosis. |

**Late-presentation patterns:** An untreated PSC patient may present with *end-stage liver disease* without ever having had jaundice or pruritus (silent progression). The stem might describe a 45-year-old with UC who found a new abdominal wall varix and has splenomegaly. The LFTs show low albumin, prolonged INR, and cholestasis. *Don't miss the cirrhosis.* Another stem: a patient with known PSC who stops attending follow-up, then presents with *acute cholangitis and coma* (hepatic encephalopathy from sepsis). High mortality.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**
- **ABBC** – for strictures: **A**utoimmune (PSC, IgG4), **B**acterial (recurrent pyogenic cholangitis), **B**enign (ischemic, surgical trauma), **C**ancer (cholangiocarcinoma, pancreatic cancer). *Use this to avoid anchoring on PSC.*
- **PSC:** **P**ancreatobiliary **S**trictures **C**holangitis – but primary is *intra + extrahepatic, not just biliary.*
- **SAME:** **S**trictures, **A**LP ↑, **M**iddle-aged men, **E**xclude IgG4.

**Clinical Pearls (pattern recognition):**
1. **Pruritus in a patient with IBD is PSC until proven otherwise** – check ALP and MRCP.
2. **ALP elevation >10x normal with ALT <100 in an IBD patient?** The odds are >90% PSC (or primary biliary cholangitis if AMA positive).
3. **A "dominant stricture" (CBD >1.5mm on MRCP) in a known PSC patient is malignant until proven innocent** – needs ERCP brushings every 6-12 months.
4. **If a patient with PSC gets jaundice without cholangitis, think dominant stricture or CCA** – not just progression of disease.
5. **PSC can present years before IBD** – always ask about bowel symptoms, but don't rely on them. A normal colonoscopy doesn't exclude future IBD.
6. **Small-duct PSC (normal MRCP, biopsy positive) has a **better prognosis** than classic PSC** – lower risk of CCA, less need for transplant.
7. **Liver biopsy is rarely needed for PSC diagnosis** – reserve for small-duct suspicion or when MRCP is contraindicated.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- **Ordering ERCP "for diagnosis"** – use MRCP first. ERCP carries 10-30% risk of pancreatitis in PSC patients (papilla manipulation).
- **Giving high-dose UDCA** (already covered – dangerous).
- **Treating itch with antihistamines** (ineffective) instead of cholestyramine or UDCA.
- **Using oral vitamin K when coagulopathy is due to liver failure, not deficiency** – check INR response after IM vitamin K.
- **Missing CCA** – a rise in CA19-9 in a PSC patient is not always cancer, but >100 warrants prompt MRCP/ERCP.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior or ICU:**
- **NEWS2 ≥ 7** (or ≥5 with any single red parameter) – concern for cholangitis/sepsis
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** – risk of septic shock
- **Systolic BP <90 mmHg** despite 30 mL/kg fluid bolus → possible ascending cholangitis with septic shock → ICU
- **GCS ≤14** or acute confusion → hepatic encephalopathy or sepsis-associated delirium
- **Acute variceal bleed:** massive hematemesis or melena, tachycardia, hypotension → call gastro/bleeding team, massive transfusion protocol, ICU if unstable.
- **Rising bilirubin >10 mg/dL in <2 weeks** with right upper quadrant pain and fever → urgent ERCP needed (call hepatobiliary consultant)
- **Anuria or creatinine >250 µmol/L** – hepatorenal syndrome (type 1) is an ICU indication for vasopressors and possibly terlipressin/albumin.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **MELD score >15** → consider transplant referral; >20 → urgent transplant evaluation.
- **INR >2.5** despite vitamin K → liver failure, not deficiency; call senior.
- **Platelets <50 x10⁹/L** with active bleeding → platelet transfusion + consider splenic artery embolization if hypersplenism.
- **Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP):** ascitic fluid PMN >250 cells/µL → IV albumin + antibiotics.

**Remember:** PSC patients are immunosuppressed if they have IBD on biologics or post-transplant – lower threshold for sepsis. Any decompensation (acute on chronic liver failure) should trigger immediate multidisciplinary team (hepatology, transplant surgery, ICU) discussion.

---

**Final teaching points:** PSC is not just a liver disease – it's a systemic condition with bowel, bone, and biliary malignancy risks. Your job as a junior doctor is to recognise the pattern early, avoid harmful therapies (high-dose UDCA, unnecessary ERCP), ensure vitamin supplementation, and keep a low threshold for cholangiocarcinoma surveillance. The two words to remember: **stagnation and stricture.** Keep the bile flowing – because stagnation breeds infection and cancer.
